questions.question: In the static site generator model, when is the HTML for a blog post produced?
choices: On every visitor request, by a Ruby process on the server
Once at build time, before the site is deployed
In the browser, from a JSON API response
Never - the Markdown is served directly to the browser
questions.explanation: An SSG renders content into static HTML at build time. The output files are then served as-is, with no per-request rendering on the server.

questions.question: You're asked to build an internal dashboard that shows the team's live PagerDuty queue, updated every few seconds and personalised to each user. Is a Jekyll static site the right fit?
choices: Yes - Jekyll can rebuild every few seconds via a cron job
Yes - JavaScript on the page can fetch the data, so static works fine
No - per-user, real-time content is a poor match for build-time rendering
No - static sites cannot serve any JavaScript at all
questions.explanation: Per-user and real-time content is exactly the case build-time rendering handles badly. A server-rendered or API-backed app is a better choice; Jekyll is for content that's the same for every visitor between builds.

questions.question: Which trade-off is the strongest argument for choosing Jekyll + GitHub Pages over a server-rendered framework for a personal engineering blog?
choices: It supports more interactive features out of the box than a server-rendered stack
Posts live as Markdown in Git, deploys are pushes, and hosting is free with no runtime to maintain
It renders pages faster on every request because Ruby is faster than Node
It makes per-user authentication trivial compared to other stacks
questions.explanation: The pairing's value is operational: text files in Git, push-to-deploy, and free hosting with no app server. Static sites don't win on interactivity or auth - those have to come from elsewhere.
